heroTitle: Learn the Tamil you can actually say at home.
heroText: A first lesson for English-dominant learners who want real conversation with family, without needing Tamil script.
panelList: How to answer a parent when you get home
How to ask for food or water politely
How to ask someone to repeat or slow down
title: Coming home and talking to family
description: The lesson stays short on purpose. Listen, repeat, choose the best reply, then do a quick recall round. This version uses Chennai-leaning spoken Tamil that sounds natural in many urban home conversations.

audio.note: This website is ready for hosted Chennai-style MP3s. Add your recordings to /audio/lesson-01/ to replace browser-generated playback.

phrases.useCase: A common way a parent or sibling may check in when you arrive home.
phrases.note: The literal translation sounds blunt in English, but in Tamil it often feels caring and normal.

phrases.useCase: Use this to answer someone asking if you just came in.
phrases.note: The phrasing here is meant to feel like urban home Tamil from Chennai rather than formal textbook Tamil.

phrases.useCase: A very common home question that often signals care, not just literal curiosity.
phrases.note: Learners should recognize that this can function almost like a warm greeting at home.

phrases.useCase: A natural answer when someone asks if you already ate.
phrases.note: The casual ending here is common in speech.

phrases.useCase: Use at home when asking whether food is available.
phrases.note: This works best in close family settings, not formal situations.

phrases.useCase: A polite request to a parent, elder, or host at home.
phrases.note: The respectful ending matters here; it keeps the tone soft and appropriate.

phrases.meaning: Please say it a little more slowly.
phrases.useCase: Use when someone speaks too fast and you need help.
phrases.note: This is especially useful for heritage learners who understand some Tamil but miss fast speech.

phrases.useCase: A quick way to say you missed what someone said.
phrases.note: This is one of the highest-value survival phrases in the entire app.

phrases.useCase: Use when someone is cooking, cleaning, or getting something ready.
phrases.note: Code-mixing with English words like 'help' is especially common in Chennai and other urban bilingual homes.

phrases.useCase: A natural response when someone calls you from another room.
phrases.note: Short, everyday replies like this make learners sound more natural very quickly.

phrases.useCase: Use this to compliment food, tea, or something prepared at home.
phrases.note: This is useful because appreciation language matters in family settings.

phrases.useCase: A common modern mixed-language home reply.
phrases.note: Many Chennai households naturally mix English into spoken Tamil, so this sounds familiar and current.

dialogues.context: She is checking in after you came home from work. Which reply sounds natural if you have not eaten yet?
dialogues.explanation: This answer directly responds to the food question in natural spoken Tamil.

dialogues.prompt: An elder at home is speaking too fast.
dialogues.context: You understood part of the sentence but need them to repeat it more slowly. What should you say?
dialogues.explanation: This is respectful and practical. It asks them to slow down without sounding abrupt.

dialogues.prompt: You walk in and your dad asks, 'vandhutiya?'
dialogues.context: You want to answer naturally and let him know you just got in.
dialogues.explanation: This reply fits the moment and sounds like a normal family exchange.

dialogues.prompt: You want to compliment the food at dinner.
dialogues.context: Which phrase is the best warm, family-friendly response?
dialogues.explanation: This is a broad compliment you can use often, especially for food made at home.

recalls.note: Choose the respectful ending when speaking to an elder or host.
A core phrase for staying calm in conversation.
Use this after someone checks that you came back.
A warm phrase to compliment food or tea at home.
